gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Protein engineering is limited by the complexity of exploring vast design spaces and the difficulty of generating thermodynamically stable variants. Directed Evolution is laborious, and existing generative models often produce non-functional proteins because they cannot condition generation on thermodynamic stability. The authors hypothesize that learning the sequence-to-free-energy relationship from a small set of biophysical simulations using a VAE can overcome these limitations. They develop PREVENT to generate stable, functional variants by approximating the free energy landscape, enabling both controlled generation of low-energy variants and energy prediction for prioritization.

section_gists.heading: Protein engineering using variational free energy approximation
section_gists.gist: Introduces the challenge of protein engineering: vast design space, need for thermodynamic stability. Directed Evolution is laborious; computational methods are limited by cost of free energy calculations. Generative deep learning can learn from sequence/structure data but often fails to produce stable variants. Hypothesis: learning sequence-to-free-energy relationship from a small set of biophysical simulations using a VAE can overcome this. PREVENT model proposed: generates variants with minimal free energy and predicts free energy for prioritization. Evaluated on EcNAGK, a conformationally dynamic homodimer. Designed 40 variants; 85% functional, 55% with similar growth to wildtype despite up to 9 mutations.
process_signals: Problem: protein engineering requires exploring vast design space and ensuring thermodynamic stability.
Limitation of existing methods: DE is laborious; computational biophysical methods are costly; generative models don't condition on stability.
Hypothesis: VAE can learn sequence-to-free-energy relationship from a small set of simulations.
Solution: PREVENT model for controlled generation of low-energy variants and energy prediction.
Test case: EcNAGK, chosen for its complex thermodynamic landscape.
Key result: 85% of 40 designed variants functional, 55% with wildtype-like growth.
methods: Variational Autoencoder (VAE) to learn sequence-to-free-energy relationship
In silico mutagenesis and free energy estimation (FOLDX) to create training data
Two design strategies: Prior Optimization of free Energy (POE) and Seeded Non-optimal free Energy ranking (SNE)
metrics: Growth rate comparison to wildtype (55% similar)
failures_or_limits: Not all variants completely recovered wildtype growth phenotype (only 55% similar).
Some variants did not produce viable transformants (7 out of 40).

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: Describes computational experiments: in silico mutagenesis of EcNAGK generated 117,387 unique variants with up to 38 mutations (~15% residues). Free energy computed with FOLDX; most mutations destabilizing, positive correlation between mutations and ΔG. Dataset split 90/10 train/test. Subsampled training sets (25K, 50K, 75K) to study data size effect. Transformer VAE with 128-dim latent space, trained 1400 epochs, batch size 256, lr 1e-4, dropout 0.2, 25% masking. Evaluated reconstruction error and RMSE of free energy. Full dataset: reconstruction error 114.80 (perplexity 0.66), RMSE 9.27. With 25K: RMSE 12.12. Spearman correlation high (0.96 full, 0.92 25K). Concordance at top >80% for top 1000. Latent space analysis: 126/128 components active; PCA shows lower energy sequences central, higher energy peripheral (folding funnel).
process_signals: Training data generation: random mutagenesis, FOLDX energy estimation.
Model training: transformer VAE, hyperparameters chosen.
Performance evaluation: reconstruction error, RMSE, Spearman correlation, CAT.
Observation: model robust to training set size; energy prediction correlates well with FOLDX.
Latent space organization resembles folding funnel, validating learned energy landscape.
methods: In silico mutagenesis: random mutations up to 15% residues, discard duplicates.
Free energy estimation: FOLDX empirical force field.
Transformer VAE with 128-dim latent space, 25% masking.
Evaluation: reconstruction error, RMSE, Spearman correlation, concordance at top (CAT).
Latent space analysis: component activation (std >0.1), PCA.
failures_or_limits: RMSE increases with smaller training sets (9.27 to 12.12).
Predicted energy values may differ from FOLDX, but ranking is robust.

section_gists.heading: Hyperparameter analysis
section_gists.gist: Analyzed effect of latent dimension, transformer size, and training epochs. Smaller model (4 encoder layers, 2 decoder layers, 256 embedding, 4 heads) and original (8 encoder, 4 decoder, 512 embedding, 8 heads) tested. Training for 500 epochs sufficient; most models converge 100-200 epochs. Latent dimension doesn't affect reconstruction error. Model size and latent dimension affect RMSE: smaller model with 128-dim latent achieved lowest RMSE 6.84. Spearman correlation remained high (0.97-0.98) across all models.
process_signals: Hyperparameter tuning: latent dimension, model size, epochs.
Finding: reconstruction error insensitive to latent dimension; RMSE affected by model size and latent dimension.
Smaller model with 128-dim latent gave best RMSE (6.84).
Spearman correlation consistently high, so original model used for downstream tasks.
methods: Compared two transformer configurations: original (8 enc, 4 dec, 512 emb, 8 heads) and smaller (4 enc, 2 dec, 256 emb, 4 heads).
Varied latent dimensions (not specified, but 128 used).
Evaluated on held-out test set.
failures_or_limits: Original model had higher RMSE (9.27) than smaller model (6.84), but original used for downstream tasks due to high correlation.

section_gists.heading: Sequence analysis of the EcNAGK variants generated by PREVENT
section_gists.gist: Generated variants using two strategies: POE (2000 samples) and SNE (30,000 samples). Filtered out training set duplicates, binding site mutations, duplicates. Obtained 1282 POE (66%) and 13,323 SNE (44%) new variants. POE variants more diverse (avg 7.09 mutations) than SNE (3.57). Only ~25% mutations conservative (BLOSUM62). Mutation types similar across strategies, but locations differ: POE mutates C-terminus (ATP-binding domain) with non-conservative mutations (e.g., D212W), SNE mutations uniform. PREVENT selects energetically favorable mutations regardless of evolutionary conservation.
process_signals: Generation strategies: POE explores free energy landscape freely; SNE generates near wildtype.
Filtering criteria: remove training set duplicates, binding site mutations, duplicates.
Observation: POE yields more diverse variants with more mutations.
Surprising result: only 25% mutations conservative; PREVENT may ignore evolutionary information.
Mutation location bias: POE targets C-terminus, SNE uniform.
methods: POE: prior optimization of free energy (constrained trust region method).
SNE: seeded non-optimal free energy ranking (encode seed, decode, rank by predicted energy).
Sequence analysis: BLOSUM62 for conservation, mutation distribution.
failures_or_limits: Low fraction of conservative mutations suggests model may not capture evolutionary constraints.
POE may introduce radical mutations in critical regions (e.g., ATP-binding domain).

section_gists.gist: Built library for experimental validation: 10 lowest free energy from POE, 10 from SNE, plus 10 highest ELBO (SNL) and 10 highest identity to wildtype (SNI) for comparison. Average free energy: SNE -29.8 kcal/mol, SNL -18 kcal/mol. (Section truncated, but process clear: selection of variants for wet-lab testing.)
process_signals: Library design: 40 variants total, 10 each from POE, SNE, SNL, SNI.
Comparison strategies: energy-driven (POE, SNE) vs. common generative strategies (ELBO, identity).
Energy range: SNE lowest, SNL highest.

metrics: Average free energy: SNE -29.8 kcal/mol, SNL -18 kcal/mol.

section_gists.heading: Establishing the auxotrophic screen for EcNAGK
section_gists.gist: EcNAGK (argB) is conditionally essential; argB knockouts can be rescued by expressing functional EcNAGK via plasmid. Initial expression with pKCHU-argB caused slow growth (>24h lag) due to overexpression. Recoded argB using E. coli codon bias, achieving 25-fold reduction in expression (RT-qPCR) and drastic growth improvement. Decided to backtranslate all variants using same codon bias strategy.
process_signals: Experimental setup: auxotrophic screen in M9 minimal media without arginine.
Problem: initial overexpression caused severe growth penalty.
Solution: codon optimization reduced expression 25-fold, improved growth.
Decision: apply codon optimization to all variants.
methods: Auxotrophic screen: BW25113 ΔargB strain, complementation with plasmid-borne variants.
Codon optimization using E. coli codon usage bias.
RT-qPCR to measure expression reduction.
failures_or_limits: Initial overexpression system caused >24h lag phase, penalizing cell growth.

section_gists.heading: Screening the EcNAGK variant library
section_gists.gist: 40 variants cloned into pKCHU, transformed into ΔargB using high-throughput OT-2 robot. 7 variants (poe4, snl3, snl6, snl7, sni10, sne1, sne3) did not produce viable transformants. 33 viable; 22 quickly recovered phenotype in minimal media, 6 slow/intermediate, 5 no recovery. Total 28 active (85% of 33 viable, or 70% of all 40). Energy-based strategies (POE+SNE) gave 14 functional variants with avg 5.64 mutations vs 1.64 for sequence-only (SNI+SNL). ELBO prioritization (SNL) yielded fewest viable variants. Top candidates (poe1, snl1, sni1, sne2) growth assay: poe1, sni1, sne2 similar to wildtype; sne2 had shorter lag; snl1 39% slower growth. Mutations not biased to surface; ~33% in hydrophobic regions. snl1 has radical Gly123 mutation (text cut off).
process_signals: Transformation: 7 variants failed to produce viable transformants (possible toxicity or lethality).
Functional assay: 28/40 (70%) active, but 85% of viable ones.
Energy-based strategies yield more diverse functional variants (more mutations).
ELBO prioritization performed worst.
Growth rates: most top variants similar to wildtype, except snl1 slower.
Mutation location: not surface-biased.
methods: High-throughput heat-shock transformation with OT-2 robot.
Auxotrophic selection on M9 minimal agar without arginine.
Growth curve assay in liquid minimal media.
Hydropathy analysis for mutation location.
metrics: Growth rates: snl1 39% slower; sne2 23-34% shorter lag.
failures_or_limits: snl1 (highest ELBO) had reduced growth rate.
ELBO strategy yielded fewest viable variants.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: PREVENT addresses complexity of protein folding by learning free energy landscape from few simulations. Enables generation and energy prediction; free energy minimization over latent space allows rapid identification of diverse functional variants. Cost of energy prediction constant with mutations, unlike direct FOLDX. EcNAGK redesign: 85% functional, but not all fully recovered wildtype growth. Energy-based selection yields more diverse functional proteins. Useful for drug discovery (bioequivalence). Limitations: FOLDX accuracy for large/unfolded proteins; may need other biophysical engines; ΔΔG might be more robust; model explainability needs work. Framework can learn other biochemical properties.
process_signals: Key advantage: constant cost of energy prediction vs. mutations.
Validation: 85% functional, but incomplete recovery for some.
Energy information improves diversity of functional variants.
Limitations acknowledged: FOLDX reliability, need for other force fields, ΔΔG vs ΔG, explainability.
Generalizability: can learn other properties.
failures_or_limits: FOLDX may be less accurate for large proteins or unfolded states.
Relative free energy changes (ΔΔG) might be more robust.
Not all functional variants fully recovered wildtype growth.

section_gists.heading: In silico mutagenesis and free energy estimation
section_gists.gist: Generated EcNAGK variants by random mutagenesis (except first Met), up to 15% residues mutated, discarding duplicates. Estimated Gibbs free energy using FoldX: repaired wildtype structure, then BUILDMODEL with default parameters, averaging 2 runs. PREVENT extends VAE to two observed variables: sequence x and ΔG g, conditioned on latent z. Objective: maximize ELBO with reconstruction terms for sequence and energy, plus KL divergence. (Text cut off, but core method described.)

Model: VAE with two decoders (sequence and energy).
Objective: maximize ELBO with reconstruction losses for both modalities.
methods: Random mutagenesis: uniform random mutations, up to 15% residues.
Free energy estimation: FoldX REPAIRPDB and BUILDMODEL, average 2 runs.
VAE with two observed variables: sequence and ΔG.
ELBO with reconstruction terms for sequence and energy.

section_gists.heading: Protein engineering using variational energy approximation
section_gists.gist: PREVENT implements two design strategies: SNE (seeded non-optimal energy ranking) and POE (prior optimization of free energy). SNE: encode seed, decode sequence, then estimate energy by passing generated sequence through encoder and energy decoder (N=300 samples). POE: minimize predicted free energy over latent space using constrained trust region method (within 3 std of mean), then decode optimal z to generate sequences, ranked by expected energy. POE aims to explore design space more efficiently and generate more diverse proteins.
process_signals: Two strategies: SNE for ranking, POE for optimization-driven generation.
SNE: energy prediction via Monte Carlo sampling from posterior.
POE: latent space optimization with trust region constraint to control diversity.
Hypothesis: POE navigates design space more efficiently.
methods: SNE: encode seed, decode sequence, re-encode to get energy posterior, sample N=300 to estimate E[g|x].
POE: constrained trust region method to minimize f_θ2(z), constraint: z within 3 std from mean.
Generated sequences ranked by expected energy.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The research aimed to overcome the limitation of generative models in producing thermodynamically stable proteins by integrating free energy approximation into a VAE framework.

The initial hypothesis was that a VAE could learn the sequence-to-free-energy relationship from a small set of FOLDX simulations, enabling both generation of low-energy variants and energy prediction.
The choice of EcNAGK as a test case was motivated by its complex thermodynamic landscape, providing a challenging validation.
The two design strategies (POE and SNE) were developed to leverage the learned energy landscape in different ways: POE for de novo optimization, SNE for ranking seeded variants.
The experimental validation via auxotrophic screen was designed to directly test functionality in vivo, with the expectation that energy-based designs would yield more diverse functional variants.

The observation that only ~25% of mutations were conservative suggested that PREVENT prioritizes energetic stability over evolutionary conservation, which was a surprising but potentially advantageous feature.

The failure of some variants to transform or rescue growth indicated that predicted low energy does not guarantee in vivo functionality, possibly due to factors like expression toxicity or folding kinetics.
The comparison with ELBO and identity-based selection confirmed that energy information improves diversity without sacrificing functionality, supporting the core hypothesis.
The limitations acknowledged (FOLDX accuracy, need for ΔΔG, explainability) suggest future directions for improving the framework.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Protein engineering is limited by the complexity of exploring vast design spaces and the difficulty of generating thermodynamically stable variants. Existing generative models often produce non-functional proteins because they cannot condition generation on thermodynamic stability.

canonical_events.summary: Protein engineering requires exploring a vast design space and ensuring thermodynamic stability, which is challenging. Existing generative models often produce non-functional variants because they do not condition on stability.

Developed PREVENT model, extending VAE to two observed variables (sequence and ΔG) with separate decoders, trained by maximizing ELBO with reconstruction terms for both. Uses transformer architecture.
Generated training data: in silico random mutagenesis of EcNAGK (up to 15% residues), yielding 117,387 unique variants. Computed free energy ΔG using FOLDX.
Trained transformer VAE on mutagenesis dataset with 128-dim latent space, achieving reconstruction error 114.80 and RMSE 9.27 on full dataset. Spearman correlation 0.96.
Latent space analysis revealed 126/128 active components and a folding funnel organization with lower energy sequences central, validating learned energy landscape.
Hyperparameter analysis: smaller model with 128-dim latent achieved lowest RMSE 6.84, but Spearman correlation remained high (0.97-0.98) across all models. Original model used for downstream tasks.
Generated variants using two strategies: POE (prior optimization of free energy) and SNE (seeded non-optimal energy ranking). After filtering, obtained 1282 new variants from POE and 13,323 from SNE.
Sequence analysis: POE variants more diverse (avg 7.09 mutations) than SNE (3.57). Only ~25% mutations conservative. POE targets C-terminus with non-conservative mutations; SNE mutations uniform.
Designed a library of 40 EcNAGK variants for experimental validation: 10 lowest free energy from POE, 10 from SNE, plus 10 highest ELBO (SNL) and 10 highest identity (SNI) for comparison.
Initial plasmid-based expression of wildtype argB caused severe growth penalty (>24h lag) due to overexpression, requiring codon optimization.
Recoded argB using E. coli codon bias, achieving 25-fold reduction in expression and drastic growth improvement. Applied codon optimization to all variants.
Transformed 40 variants into ΔargB strain using high-throughput OT-2 robot. 7 variants failed to produce viable transformants; 33 viable, 28 functional (85% of viable, 70% of all).

Energy-based strategies (POE+SNE) yielded 14 functional variants with avg 5.64 mutations vs 1.64 for sequence-only (SNI+SNL). ELBO prioritization (SNL) performed worst. Growth assays: most top variants similar to wildtype, except snl1 39% slower.

Computational structural analysis (ESMFOLD, OPENMM, eNMA, ESM-IF) confirmed variants closely resemble wildtype structure (avg pLDDT 89.64, avg RMSD 1.70Å) and are consistent with wildtype fold, though slightly less stable.
FOLDX accuracy limited for large proteins or unfolded states; relative free energy changes (ΔΔG) might be more robust. Model explainability not addressed.

source.title: Protein engineering using variational free energy approximation

initial_request.research_problem: Protein engineering is limited by the complexity of exploring vast design spaces and the difficulty of generating thermodynamically stable variants. Existing generative models often produce non-functional proteins because they cannot condition generation on thermodynamic stability.
initial_request.known_context: Generative models for protein design typically focus on sequence diversity but neglect thermodynamic stability, leading to many non-functional variants.
constraints: Mutations should not disrupt binding sites
quantifiable_goals: Generate functional EcNAGK variants with improved or maintained stability
Achieve high experimental viability and functionality rates

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Protein engineering requires exploring a vast design space and ensuring thermodynamic stability, which is challenging. Existing generative models often produce non-functional variants because they do not condition on stability.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to generate protein variants that are both diverse and thermodynamically stable?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A variational autoencoder that jointly models sequence and free energy can learn a latent representation that captures the sequence-to-stability relationship, enabling the generation of stable variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a model that extends VAE to two observed variables (sequence and ΔG) with separate decoders, trained by maximizing ELBO with reconstruction terms for both.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The PREVENT model is designed to allow controlled generation of variants with minimal free energy and prediction of free energy for generated variants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Model design requires training data, so in silico mutagenesis and free energy estimation are performed.

evidence.quote_or_span: Engineering proteins is a challenging task requiring the exploration of a vast design space.
most models do not generate thermodynamically stable proteins, thus leading to many non-functional variants.

we developed the PRotein Engineering by Variational frEe eNergy approximation (PREVENT) model, which allows both the controlled generation of variants with minimal free energy as well as the prediction of the free energy associated with generated variants for downstream experimental prioritization.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Training dataset of 117,387 variants with sequences and FOLDX ΔG values.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can the transformer VAE learn a meaningful latent representation that captures the sequence-to-free-energy relationship?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Training a transformer VAE with 128-dim latent space on the full dataset will yield low reconstruction error and high Spearman correlation between predicted and true ΔG.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Train transformer VAE on full dataset with 128-dim latent space.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Reconstruction error 114.80, RMSE 9.27, Spearman correlation 0.96 on full dataset.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: After training, latent space is analyzed to validate the learned energy landscape.

evidence.quote_or_span: the reconstruction error was 114.80 (perplexity: 0.66) for the full dataset
obtained the best results when using the full dataset (RMSE = 9.27)
96$ when training on the full dataset and only dropping to $\rho = 0.92$ when using only 25K sequences, suggesting that predicted values are a robust metric for variants ranking.

source.title: Protein engineering using variational free energy approximation

initial_request.research_problem: Protein engineering is limited by the complexity of exploring vast design spaces and the difficulty of generating thermodynamically stable variants. Existing generative models often produce non-functional proteins because they cannot condition generation on thermodynamic stability.
initial_request.known_context: Generative models for protein design typically focus on sequence diversity but neglect thermodynamic stability, leading to many non-functional variants.
constraints: Mutations should not disrupt binding sites
quantifiable_goals: Generate functional EcNAGK variants with improved or maintained stability
Achieve high experimental viability and functionality rates

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Protein engineering requires exploring a vast design space and ensuring thermodynamic stability, which is challenging. Existing generative models often produce non-functional variants because they do not condition on stability.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to generate protein variants that are both diverse and thermodynamically stable?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A variational autoencoder that jointly models sequence and free energy can learn a latent representation that captures the sequence-to-stability relationship, enabling the generation of stable variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a model that extends VAE to two observed variables (sequence and ΔG) with separate decoders, trained by maximizing ELBO with reconstruction terms for both.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The PREVENT model is designed to allow controlled generation of variants with minimal free energy and prediction of free energy for generated variants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Model design requires training data, so in silico mutagenesis and free energy estimation are performed.

evidence.quote_or_span: Engineering proteins is a challenging task requiring the exploration of a vast design space.
most models do not generate thermodynamically stable proteins, thus leading to many non-functional variants.

we developed the PRotein Engineering by Variational frEe eNergy approximation (PREVENT) model, which allows both the controlled generation of variants with minimal free energy as well as the prediction of the free energy associated with generated variants for downstream experimental prioritization.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Training dataset of 117,387 variants with sequences and FOLDX ΔG values.

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Training a transformer VAE with 128-dim latent space on the full dataset will yield low reconstruction error and high Spearman correlation between predicted and true ΔG.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Train transformer VAE on full dataset with 128-dim latent space.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Reconstruction error 114.80, RMSE 9.27, Spearman correlation 0.96 on full dataset.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: After training, latent space is analyzed to validate the learned energy landscape.

evidence.quote_or_span: the reconstruction error was 114.80 (perplexity: 0.66) for the full dataset
obtained the best results when using the full dataset (RMSE = 9.27)
96$ when training on the full dataset and only dropping to $\rho = 0.92$ when using only 25K sequences, suggesting that predicted values are a robust metric for variants ranking.

With our procedure, we generated 117,387 unique variants, with each variant harbouring up to 38 mutations ( $\approx$ 15% of the residues), and then computed the associated free energy, $\Delta G$ , using FOLDX $^{14}$ .
